jobs.scope: Internal quality of the spec file alone, no cross-level comparison.
checks: Terminology stability (activity, command, skill, tier, gate, phase, mode) — one term = one meaning throughout
16 principles (P1-P16) consistently cited and applied across their appearances
Modes (Micro / Lightweight / Full) remain logically distinct with no overlap
Gates uniform in severity (each Gate has a defined threshold)
Internal numeric consistency (if §1.6 says 23 commands, §12 TOC must match)
Internal cross-references resolve (e.g., §4.2 referenced → §4.2 exists)
No TODO/FIXME/TBD markers
Tables well-formed (consistent column counts, alignment, headers)

jobs.scope: Internal quality of the design doc alone.
checks: Pattern consistency across §5.x subsections (similar length, structure, depth)
Rationale documented for each significant decision
No self-contradictions (decisions that contradict each other)
§6 tables consistent (counts, column alignment, content)
Internal cross-references resolve
No TODO/FIXME/TBD markers
Tables well-formed

jobs.scope: All 23 activity skills follow a uniform structural skeleton and tone.
checks: YAML frontmatter present with a 'description' field
Standard sections present (Options / Prerequisites / Workflow or Phases)
User-facing message tone consistent across activities
Gate decisions formatted identically (same wording patterns)
References to agents, templates, tools use consistent path conventions
Phase/step numbering uniform
Error message format consistent

jobs.scope: All agents (orchestrator + 10 specialized) follow a uniform structure.
checks: YAML frontmatter (name, description) present
Standard sections (Role / Activated by / Perspective / Required readings / Core Principles / Anti-patterns / Output format)
Tone consistent (neutral, directive, cite patterns not judgment)
Activation triggers match the activities that reference them
Each specialized agent declares its 'Activated by' clearly
Anti-patterns formatted as NEVER/ALWAYS statements uniformly

jobs.scope: All Python tools follow uniform quality standards (software engineering hygiene).
checks: Header '# @gse-tool <name> <version>' on line 2 (regardless of file location)
Module docstring present and clear (purpose + usage example)
Public functions documented with docstrings
Error handling pattern consistent (return dict with 'status' field OR raise typed exceptions)
CLI pattern uniform (argparse + subcommands where applicable)
Stdlib-only by default; optional dependencies documented and gracefully degraded
No print() in library code; CLI output goes through a rendering function

jobs.scope: All templates complete, consistent, and declared in MANIFEST.yaml.
checks: YAML syntax valid (fallback to structural check if PyYAML unavailable)
JSON syntax valid
Dockerfile templates contain 'ARG SOURCE_COMMIT'
.env example files contain all documented keys with placeholder values
Each template listed in MANIFEST.yaml with correct metadata
Templates referenced by activities actually exist
No orphan templates (files not referenced by any activity or agent)

jobs.scope: Reference docs accurate, well-structured, and kept fresh.
checks: Tables well-formed (prices, types, endpoints)
'last verified YYYY-MM-DD' markers present for volatile data
No stale information older than 180 days without update
Canonical patterns (SSH, CLI invocations) match what tools and skills expect
Self-contained (a forker can read without needing external context)

jobs.scope: Design is a proper refinement of spec. Both directions checked: design realizes spec AND design may reveal spec improvements.
checks: Every spec principle (P1-P16) has a corresponding design implementation or explicit reference
Every design decision traces to a spec requirement
Numeric claims align (agent counts, command counts, mode counts)
Terminology coherent across both files
No design decision contradicts spec
[bidirectional] If design contains precision or clarity absent from spec, propose an upward update
[bidirectional] If spec lacks a concept that design assumes, flag as spec gap

jobs.scope: Orchestrator + 16 principles + their spec/design references coherent as the governance core.
checks: Orchestrator embodies all 16 principles in its structure
Each principle is activatable at runtime (has a practical application)
Cross-platform parity (Claude / Cursor / opencode) described in design matches code
Spec's orchestrator description matches the markdown content
Specialized agent invocations consistent across all activities
[bidirectional] If orchestrator content reveals a missing principle in spec, propose addition

jobs.scope: Entire deployment ecosystem coherent horizontally + aligned with spec deploy row and design §5.18.
checks: Every concept in deploy.md has a matching description in design §5.18
Deploy-operator principles reflected in skill phases
Dockerfile templates match what the skill documents (ports, healthchecks, CMD)
References consulted at documented points in the skill
Python tools match the skill's invocation contracts (subcommands, args)
Deploy state schema matches all referenced fields
Training mode flow (init, distribute, reap) coherent across all files
No file in the cluster contradicts another
[bidirectional] If tools expose capabilities not documented in skill or design, propose upward update

jobs.scope: Sprint lifecycle activities + their templates + their spec/design references coherent as an end-to-end flow.
checks: Phase transitions LC00 → LC01 → LC02 → LC03 consistent across all activities
TASK state machine (planned → in-progress → review → fixing → done) coherent
Templates referenced correctly (no drift between activity and template schema)
Gate sequences uniform across activities
DEC-NNN decision format applied consistently
Handoffs between activities well-defined (what state each produces for the next)

jobs.scope: All state files + activities that read/write them + their schema references coherent.
checks: Each activity that writes state matches the template schema
Field names consistent (e.g., last_activity_timestamp everywhere, not _date)
State transitions documented in activity workflows
MANIFEST.yaml lists all templates with correct paths
No orphan field (written by an activity but never read)
No orphan template (not referenced by any activity)

jobs.scope: Cross-cutting activities (orchestration, onboarding, learning, health) coherent with spec and design.
checks: /gse:go decision logic matches design §5.14
/gse:hug profile detection algorithm uniform
/gse:learn → profile.yaml topics schema used
/gse:health → 8 dimensions defined consistently
Integration between activities (hug → go, learn → status) well-described
Profile calibration (coach axis 2) leverages profile.yaml correctly

jobs.scope: Coach agent + pedagogy activities + profile schema coherent as a learning subsystem.
checks: 8 coach axes (pedagogy, profile_calibration, sprint_velocity, workflow_health, quality_trends, engagement_pattern, process_deviation, sustainability) implemented
P14 preambles format consistent across invocations
LRN-NNN format applied uniformly
Profile topics schema supports all referenced learning signals
Coach triggers (activity start, sprint close, Gate end, etc.) documented

jobs.scope: Review + tests + produce + their 7 invoked agents coherent as the QA subsystem.
checks: /gse:review spawns the correct 5 specialized agents (code-reviewer, security-auditor, requirements-analyst, architect, test-strategist) + devil-advocate
Agent invocations documented in the activity
Test Review Layering (3 tiers: STRATEGY, TST-SPEC, IMPL) applied
RVW-NNN, TST-NNN, REQ-NNN formats consistent
Each specialized agent's checklist align with its activity's workflow
Fix workflow matches findings structure

jobs.scope: End-of-sprint delivery + capitalization + upstream integration coherent as a closing flow.
checks: /gse:deliver → /gse:compound → /gse:integrate flow sequence documented
Safety backup tags (gse-backup/*) format consistent
Methodology feedback export schema matches template
Upstream repo resolution (integrate Axe 2) matches git config conventions
Compound Axes (1 capitalization, 2 upstream feedback, 3 coaching recipes) well-separated
Deliver Step 9 (freeze sprint) consistent with design §5.16

jobs.scope: Every specialized agent declares an 'Activated by:' contract listing the activities that invoke it. This job verifies the contract is bidirectional — the listed activities actually contain invocation steps for the agent. A broken contract in either direction is a bug (uncovered in v0.48.0 P4: coach claimed invocation at /gse:pause and /gse:compound but pause.md/compound.md contained zero invocation steps — 3 of 8 coach axes were silently inoperant).
checks: For each specialized agent in gse-one/src/agents/*.md, parse the 'Activated by:' declaration (in frontmatter description or the opening **Activated by:** line)

For each activity cited in 'Activated by:', grep the corresponding activity file (gse-one/src/activities/<name>.md) for an invocation step (explicit 'the orchestrator invokes the <agent-name> agent' phrase, or equivalent)
Report `error` severity for agent→activity contracts where the agent declares activation but the activity has no invocation step
Report `warning` severity for the reverse direction: activity files that invoke an agent not listed in the agent's 'Activated by:' declaration (reverse drift)

For guardrail-enforcer (Compliance archetype, cross-cutting always-active), the bidirectional check does not apply — it is invoked via hooks and tool-use interception, not via activity steps. This is an explicit exception documented in the agent file itself

For coach (Observational archetype), cross-check the Invocation contract table in coach.md — each moment row must have a corresponding invocation in the cited activity, EXCEPT the 'Any lifecycle activity start' row which is orchestrator-delegated cross-cutting (parallel to guardrail-enforcer hooks). That row carries an inline note '(orchestrator-delegated, cross-cutting — not instrumented per-activity; see gse-orchestrator.md — section Coach delegation)' and is NOT expected to have per-activity anchors. Other rows without an activity-side invocation are dead code (v0.48.0 P4 found 4 such cases)

jobs.scope: Strategic critique of the methodology design as a whole. Not about coherence, but about quality of the methodology choices themselves.
checks: Fitness for purpose: Are the 23 commands, 11 agents, 16 principles actually effective for the stated goals (Built by AI / Governed by Humans, structured SDLC for AI+human pair)?
Complexity economy: Are there mechanisms that bring little value for their cognitive cost? Redundancies? Unnecessary abstraction layers?
Principle coherence: Do the 16 principles form a coherent whole, or are some weak, rarely applicable, or conflicting?
Mode balance: Are Micro / Lightweight / Full genuinely distinct in usage, or do they overlap?
Positioning: What does GSE-One offer that other methodologies (Agile, XP, Scrum, AI-native approaches) don't? Is the differentiator clear and defended?
Design testability: Can the methodology design choices be validated empirically, or do they rest on intuition?

jobs.scope: Critique of adequacy to the current generative AI era (2024-2026): leverages AI strengths, mitigates AI risks.
checks: Leverage of LLM strengths: Does the methodology exploit current capabilities (long context, multi-file reasoning, tool use, parallelism)?
Mitigation of LLM risks: Are hallucinations, sycophancy, over-confidence, scope creep, state forgetting adequately covered by P15/P16?
Effective human governance: Is 'Governed by Humans' operationalized, or is it declarative? Are Gates sufficient?
Forward-compatibility: What risks becoming obsolete with LLM evolution (longer context, MCPs, agentic patterns)?
Agent integrity design: Is the trio devil-advocate + guardrail + review the right design to challenge AI outputs?
AI-native onboarding: Does a junior discovering AI via GSE-One acquire good reflexes, or can they develop dangerous habits (over-dependence, loss of critical thinking)?

jobs.scope: Critique of value delivered to the different user profiles.
checks: Solo novice journey: Can a developer installing GSE-One for their first project be productive in < 1 hour? Major frictions?
Expert user journey: Does an experienced developer feel constrained by the methodology, or assisted? Bypass-friendly?
Instructor journey: Is a training session with 20 learners smooth end-to-end? Likely breaking points?
Learner journey: Can a novice in a training session follow without external help?
Forker journey: Does a developer forking gensem for customization have enough support? Sufficient documentation?
Accessibility: Does the vocabulary (23 commands, 11 agents, 16 principles) create an entry barrier?
Return on investment: Is the learning curve proportional to the gain?

jobs.scope: Critique of robustness to failures, recovery, and resilience.
checks: Failure scenarios: What happens if Claude crashes mid-sprint? Network down during deploy? .gse/ corruption?
Documented recovery: Are all recovery scenarios documented and testable?
Rollback: Are all destructive actions reversible? (commits, merges, deploys, destroy)
Guardrail coherence: Do hooks cover the right critical cases without being too noisy?
Human-in-the-loop: Where can the human intervene? Where must they? Is the balance right?
Graceful degradation: If a specialized agent fails, does the system continue?
